summary: Drew DeVault celebrates one year of Sway development, an i3-compatible Wayland compositor, coinciding with the 0.9 release. Over the year, 54 contributors produced 1,823 commits and 16,601 lines of C across 515 pull requests. The project now supports most i3 features, including DeVault's own complete i3 configuration. New features since the last update include stacked and tabbed layouts, HiDPI support, and improved i3 compatibility, with packages landing in several Linux distribution repositories.
key_points: Sway reached near-complete i3 feature parity within one year of development, fulfilling its core promise as an i3-compatible Wayland compositor
The project grew to 54 contributors, with DeVault's role shifting primarily to reviewing and merging pull requests rather than writing code
New distribution packages landed in Gentoo, OpenSUSE Tumbleweed, and NixOS, signaling growing adoption
The roadmap for Sway 1.0 expanded in scope to ensure a solid release, prioritizing completeness over speed
money_quotes: Sway is an i3-compatible Wayland compositor. That is, your existing i3 configuration file will work as-is on Sway, and your keybindings and colors and fonts and for_window rules and so on will all be the same.
As of last week, all of the features from the i3 configuration I used before I started working on Sway are now supported by Sway.
Zandr Martin has started regular contributions to Sway and brought himself right up to 5th place in a short time, and while he's still learning C to boot.
Most of what I do for Sway personally is reviewing and merging pull requests.
Sway is still going very strong, and continues developing at a fast pace.
key_insight: Sway's first year demonstrated that a small open-source community could build a near-complete i3-compatible Wayland compositor, with DeVault successfully transitioning from primary coder to project steward.
